ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for water spinach as an ingredient; no exact entry for the stir-fried Thai-style preparation. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Swamp cabbage / Water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica) — stir-fried vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers water spinach as a vegetable commodity; no specific code for Thai-style stir-fried preparation. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific stir-fried vegetable dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried Thai-style kang kong weighs approximately 250 g, as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and casual Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Stir-fried Kang Kong / Water Spinach with Sambal / Sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried kang kong with sauce used as the primary reference. Values are consistent with the input record (energy 88 kcal, protein 2.0 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 6.0 g, sodium 480 mg per 100 g). Thai-style preparation (fish sauce, oyster sauce, chilli, garlic) is a close adaptation of the HPB stir-fried kang kong with sauce entry. Sodium level (480 mg/100 g) is consistent with the multi-condiment Thai preparation. All values retained from input and cross-checked against HPB reference range.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — leafy green vegetables analogous reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style stir-fried kang kong. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for leafy green vegetables and water spinach analogues, which consistently show GI values in the 10–20 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 15.0 g) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 6.0 × 2.5 = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg per 100 g from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced quantities of all high-sodium condiments (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce) to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~15), modest carbohydrate (6.0 g/100 g), and low sugar (3.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycaemic index and modest carbohydrate content, making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sauce contributes added sugar and sodium, so requesting reduced condiments is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (480 mg/100 g) offset by low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and beneficial micronutrients (vitamin C, calcium, iron)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and absence of trans fat are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from multiple condiments poses a risk; individuals with CVD should request a reduced-sauce preparation and limit portion to 150 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients (vitamin C 8 mg, calcium 40 mg) offset by high sodium and nightshade ingredients (chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kang kong provides antioxidants and anti-inflammatory polyphenols from garlic and chilli, but RA-sensitive individuals should monitor their response to nightshade solanine and request reduced high-sodium condiments to avoid worsening tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Thai-style Kang Kong (泰式炒空心菜; Thai: ผัดผักบุ้งไฟแดงสไตล์ไทย) is a popular Southeast Asian vegetable dish made from kang kong (water spinach, Ipomoea aquatica) rapidly stir-fried over high heat with garlic, fresh chilli, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants, adapted from the classic Thai street-food dish Pad Pak Boong Fai Daeng. The cooking method produces tender-crisp stems and wilted leaves with a savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-rich flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 88 kcal, 2.0 g protein, 6.0 g fat (1.0 g saturated), 6.0 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 480 mg sodium. It also contributes 40 mg calcium, 0.8 mg iron, and 8 mg vitamin C. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for most diets but its high sodium content (driven by fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce) makes it unsuitable for individuals with uncontrolled hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless prepared with reduced condiments. It is not vegetarian or vegan due to fish sauce and oyster sauce, and contains soy and shellfish allergens.

Is kang kong good for diabetics?

meal_context: shared dish in a multi-course meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for stir-fried kang kong with sauce, which is the closest approved primary source. The Thai-style preparation (fish sauce, oyster sauce, chilli, garlic) is a close variant of the HPB reference. Sodium value (480 mg/100 g) is consistent with multi-condiment stir-fried preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for leafy green vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB entry for Thai-style kang kong with this specific condiment combination was found; adaptation was required.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stir-fried Thai-style kang kong preparation is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not reported in the HPB reference; estimated ~270 mg/100 g from raw kang kong data but not confirmed for the cooked dish with condiments, so -1 retained. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this dish category as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in this vegetable stir-fry preparation per HPB reference; no hydrogenated fats used. All core macros (protein_g 2.0, fat_g 6.0, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and supported by the HPB adapted source. GI estimated at 15 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous leafy green vegetable reference; glycaemic load per 250 g serving = 2.3. Dish is not halal-certified as fish sauce and oyster sauce sourcing is unverified; halal flag set to false. Contains shellfish allergen (oyster sauce) and fish allergen (fish sauce).
